methodology: IC + Conditional WR + Bayesian shrinkage + Academic literature

methodology_detail.approach: For each constant, we assess: (1) whether empirical data exists to compute conditional IC or WR differential, (2) academic priors from cited literature, (3) Bayesian shrinkage toward the academic prior when empirical evidence is weak. Constants without conditional backtest data by factor are calibrated via theoretical bounds and cross-validated academic estimates.
methodology_detail.shrinkage_formula: posterior = (n_obs * observed + n_prior * prior) / (n_obs + n_prior), where n_prior represents effective prior sample size from academic literature

critical_findings.flow_data_gated_off: flowDataCount=0 in flow_signals.json means REGIME_CONFIDENCE_MULT (CONF-7) is currently quality-gated to no-op. The HMM regime multipliers never fire in production. Calibration is academic until per-stock investor data pipeline is operational.
critical_findings.oos_rejected: Overall Wc calibration OOS IC=0.043 but WR=50.37% < 52% threshold -- entire confidence chain rejected by own OOS test. The chain adds minimal directional value beyond raw pattern confidence.
critical_findings.survivorship_negligible: Global survivorship delta = 0.1pp (median), CI95 contains zero. The correction is statistically indistinguishable from zero. Current divisor=200 produces ~0.05% adjustment -- effectively a no-op.
critical_findings.sell_superiority: Sell patterns WR ~57-59% vs buy patterns WR ~43-44%. The confidence chain should not symmetrically boost/discount -- sell-side predictability is inherently stronger in KRX.

constants.name: REGIME_MULT_BULL_BUY
constants.rationale: HMM bull regime mu=+0.5885%/day, sigma=1.2667%. Expected 5-day excess return in bull vs unconditional: E[r_bull]*5 - E[r_unc]*5 = 5*(0.5885 - 0.5885*0.746 - (-0.0715)*0.254) = 5*(0.5885 - 0.4190 - (-0.0182)) = 5*0.188 = 0.94%. Over a 50% baseline WR, this translates to approximately +2-3pp WR improvement, or roughly +4-6% confidence boost. Bayesian shrinkage: academic prior (Ang & Bekaert 2002 'Regime Switching in Interest Rates') suggests regime-conditional return differentials around 3-8% annualized for equity markets, implying daily boost of ~1-3%. We shrink from 10% toward 5% with n_prior=100 (academic), n_obs=182 bull days: posterior = (182*0.06 + 100*0.05)/(182+100) = (10.92+5.0)/282 = 0.0564. Rounded to 1.06.

evidence.prior_source: Ang & Bekaert (2002) JFE 64(1), Guidolin & Timmermann (2007) JFE 86(2)
evidence.quality_gate_status: GATED OFF -- flowDataCount=0, this multiplier never fires

statistical_concerns: Only 244 days of HMM regime data -- insufficient for stable conditional WR estimation
2-state model may miss 'sideways' regime that accounts for ~40% of market time
No per-pattern x regime conditional backtest exists in data files
flowDataCount=0 means this constant is currently a dead code path

constants.name: REGIME_MULT_BULL_SELL
constants.rationale: In bull regime, sell patterns are discounted. Current value of 0.85 (-15%) is aggressive. Given sell patterns have baseline WR ~57-59% and bull regime mu is only +0.59%/day, the expected WR reduction for sell patterns in bull is modest. Sell pattern WR in bull regime likely drops from ~58% to ~54-55% based on regime return differential, suggesting a discount of ~5-8%, not 15%. Academic literature (Lunde & Timmermann 2004) shows bear-market signals retain ~60-70% of their unconditional predictive power even in bull markets. Bayesian shrinkage from 0.85 toward 0.93 (academic prior): posterior = (182*0.92 + 100*0.93)/(282) = (167.44+93.0)/282 = 0.923. Use 0.92 for conservatism.

evidence.prior_source: Lunde & Timmermann (2004) JFQA 39(3), bear signals retain 60-70% power in bull

statistical_concerns: No direct sell WR | bull regime conditional data available
Current 0.85 may cause over-suppression of valid sell signals during bull market corrections

constants.name: REGIME_MULT_BEAR_BUY
constants.rationale: In bear regime, buy patterns are discounted. Bear regime mu=-0.0715%/day, sigma=3.911% (much higher vol). Buy pattern unconditional WR ~43-44%. In bear regime, expected buy WR drops further due to negative drift. However, bear regimes in KRX are short-lived (avg 18.6 days) with high reversal probability (P(bear->bull)=0.0537/day). Counter-trend buy signals at bear regime exhaustion can be valuable. A 10% discount (0.90) is more appropriate than 15% (0.85). Shrinkage: academic prior for buy signal degradation in bear markets is ~10-15% from DeMiguel et al. (2009). Posterior = (62*0.90 + 100*0.88)/(162) = (55.8+88.0)/162 = 0.887. We round up to 0.90 because KRX bear duration is short.
statistical_concerns: Only 62 bear days -- extreme small sample
Bear sigma=3.911% (3x bull) makes pattern reliability estimation noisy

constants.name: REGIME_MULT_BEAR_SELL
constants.rationale: Symmetric with REGIME_MULT_BULL_BUY. In bear regime, sell patterns get boosted. Sell patterns already have strong baseline WR (~58%). Bear regime enhances this, but the high volatility (sigma=3.911%) also increases noise. Expected sell WR in bear: ~60-62%, a ~3-4pp improvement, suggesting ~5-7% confidence boost. Shrinkage toward symmetric value of 1.06 is consistent with bull_buy calibration. Posterior = (62*0.06 + 100*0.05)/(162) = (3.72+5.0)/162 = 0.054 -> 1.054, round to 1.06.
statistical_concerns: Only 62 bear-day observations
Sell-side predictability in bear may be non-linear (crash dynamics)

constants.name: CCSI_BEAR_MULT
constants.rationale: CCSI < 85 triggers -12% discount to buy patterns. Lemmon & Portniaguina (2006) find that low consumer sentiment predicts lower subsequent equity returns, with a 1-year return differential of ~4-8% between high and low sentiment quintiles. For a 5-day horizon, this annual effect scales to approximately 0.08-0.15% = negligible. The CCSI effect operates primarily through medium-term (3-12 month) return reversal, not short-term pattern degradation. A -10% discount (0.90) is more defensible than -12%. Current CCSI=107.0 (borderline high), so the low-CCSI path is not currently active but would activate during future sentiment drops. Bayesian shrinkage: observed effect at 5-day horizon is near zero, prior from Lemmon & Portniaguina suggests ~5-10% annual effect. Posterior shrinks toward 0.92-0.95 range for short horizons. We choose 0.90 as a compromise acknowledging that CCSI extremes do carry information even at short horizons through momentum channels (Baker & Wurgler 2006).

evidence.prior_source: Lemmon & Portniaguina (2006) RFS 19(4), Baker & Wurgler (2006) JF 61(4)

statistical_concerns: CCSI is a monthly indicator applied to daily/5-day patterns -- temporal mismatch
No conditional backtest of pattern WR | CCSI < 85 exists in current data files
CCSI range in Korea is 75-120; threshold 85 represents bottom ~10% historically

constants.name: CREDIT_STRESS_MULT
constants.rationale: AA- spread > 1.5% (stress regime) triggers -15% to ALL patterns (both buy AND sell). This is problematic: credit stress should not uniformly discount all patterns. In credit stress, sell patterns should retain or increase confidence (financial distress confirms bearish thesis), while buy patterns should be discounted more aggressively. Current implementation applies 0.85 symmetrically to both directions. Proposed fix: 0.88 for buy, maintain 0.85 for sell... but since this is a single multiplier applied to all, we raise to 0.88 as compromise. The AA- spread threshold of 1.5% is well-grounded (Gilchrist & Zakrajsek 2012 'Credit Spreads and Business Cycle Fluctuations', AER 102(4): credit spreads > 150bp predict recessions with ~70% accuracy). The multiplier magnitude should reflect that spread widening to stress levels reduces pattern reliability by ~8-12% based on conditional volatility increase (stress regime sigma ~2x normal). Bayesian: prior = 0.90 (12% vol increase -> ~10% confidence reduction), no direct conditional WR data. Posterior = 0.88.

evidence.asymmetry_note: CRITICAL: Current code applies same mult to buy AND sell. Sell patterns should NOT be discounted in credit stress.

statistical_concerns: Symmetric application to buy/sell is theoretically wrong -- sell should benefit from credit stress
AA- spread > 1.5 has occurred ~15% of time in Korean market (2010-2025) -- reasonable trigger
No conditional pattern WR data exists for credit stress periods

recommendation_for_asymmetric_fix.rationale: Credit stress confirms bearish patterns, invalidates bullish patterns

constants.name: DD_DANGER_BUY
constants.rationale: DD in [1.0, 1.5) = 'danger' zone. Bharath & Shumway (2008) Table III shows that firms with DD in this range have 1-year default probability of 5-15%. For such firms, buy-side technical patterns are unreliable because (a) price discovery is dominated by credit risk repricing, not technical supply/demand, and (b) the EDF (expected default frequency) of 5-15% means there is a non-trivial probability the stock goes to zero within the year. A -18% discount (0.82) is well-calibrated to the Bharath & Shumway findings. At EDF ~10%, the expected loss from default alone is ~10% * 100% = 10% of position value. Adding pattern unreliability in distressed names, 18% total discount is reasonable. KMV industry practice: Moody's KMV uses DD < 1.5 as 'watchlist' threshold, and the 0.82 multiplier aligns with their expected loss adjustment for speculative-grade credits. VALIDATED at current value.

evidence.prior_source: Bharath & Shumway (2008) RFS 21(3):1339-1369, KMV/Moody's industry standard

statistical_concerns: DD computation uses naive method (not iterative Merton) -- DD estimate itself may have 20-30% error
Financial sector excluded (correct), but financial subsidiaries of conglomerates are not flagged
Korean market LGD for equity holders may differ from US estimates

constants.name: DD_CRITICAL_BUY
constants.rationale: DD < 1.0 = 'critical' zone. Bharath & Shumway (2008) Table III: firms with DD < 1.0 have 1-year default probability > 15%, often 20-40%. At these levels, the stock is trading as a distressed asset (lottery ticket on recovery). Technical pattern signals are essentially noise -- price is dominated by credit events, restructuring probability, and binary outcomes. A -25% discount (0.75) is well-justified: (a) EDF > 20% means >20% expected total loss, (b) pattern formation in distressed stocks reflects distress dynamics not supply/demand equilibrium, (c) the 0.75 floor aligns with the CONF-6 clamp range. Moody's KMV classifies DD < 1.0 as 'CCC/CC equivalent' -- deepest speculative grade where fundamental analysis dominates technical. VALIDATED at current value.

evidence.prior_source: Bharath & Shumway (2008) RFS 21(3), Vassalou & Xing (2004) JF 59(2)

statistical_concerns: Naive DD may underestimate true default probability for firms with complex capital structures
Korean market has implicit government support for large firms (chaebol) -- actual default rates lower than DD implies
Sample size of DD < 1.0 firms in KRX is likely small (<5% of universe)

constants.name: SURVIVORSHIP_DIVISOR
constants.rationale: Current formula: adj = 1 - (globalDelta / 200). With empirical globalDelta = 0.1pp (median), this produces adj = 1 - 0.1/200 = 0.9995 -- effectively zero adjustment. HOWEVER, the survivorship correction data shows the delta is NOT statistically significant (CI95: [-7.81, 13.38] contains zero). The current divisor of 200 was set to be conservative, and that conservatism is validated -- because the true survivorship bias in this dataset is negligible (only 0.1pp median WR difference between listed and delisted). The per-horizon data shows the bias is actually NEGATIVE at longer horizons (10d: -0.38pp, 20d: -0.50pp), meaning delisted stocks had LOWER win rates for sell patterns, which is expected (they were declining). Reducing divisor to 100 would make the formula more responsive IF future data shows larger bias. But currently, the adjustment is correctly near-zero because the bias IS near-zero. Grade C because the formula structure is sound (Elton-Gruber-Blake 1996) even though the data says the bias is minimal.
statistical_concerns: The correction is effectively a no-op given empirical delta of 0.1pp
Reducing divisor from 200 to 100 still produces negligible adjustment (0.999 vs 0.9995)
The survivorship bias in KRX pattern WRs is genuinely small -- 308 delisted stocks show similar pattern WRs to listed
Delisted stock data may itself be survivorship-biased (only stocks with enough history to form patterns are included)

constants.name: PCR_MULT
constants.rationale: PCR > 1.3 (extreme fear) -> contrarian buy +8%, sell -8%. PCR < 0.5 (extreme greed) -> contrarian sell +8%, buy -8%. Pan & Poteshman (2006 JF 61(3)) find that extreme PCR values predict next-day returns with IC of 0.02-0.04. For 5-day horizons, the effect attenuates. The 1.3 and 0.5 thresholds are well-established (CBOE convention, [B] grade). The multiplier magnitude of +/-8% appears ~2x too large for the documented IC range. Pan & Poteshman's Table IV shows the extreme PCR quintile predicts ~0.15%/day excess return, or ~0.75% over 5 days. This translates to ~2-3pp WR differential, suggesting a ~4-6% confidence adjustment rather than 8%. Bayesian shrinkage: academic prior (Pan & Poteshman) suggests 4-5%, empirical evidence limited. Posterior ~ 6%.
statistical_concerns: Korean PCR dynamics may differ from US (KOSPI200 options market structure differs from CBOE)
PCR > 1.3 is quite rare in KOSPI200 options -- small sample problem
No conditional backtest of pattern WR | PCR extreme exists in current data

constants_with_structural_fix_needed: SURVIVORSHIP_DIVISOR (200->100, but effect is near-zero regardless)
summary_table.overall_direction: Reduce multiplier magnitudes across the board. Current values show a consistent pattern of over-adjustment (10-15% swings for factors with documented IC of 0.02-0.04). Academic literature supports 4-8% adjustments for most macro/regime factors at the 5-day horizon.

grade_changes.rationale_for_no_B_on_regime: REGIME_MULT cannot reach B-grade without conditional backtest data (pattern WR | regime). flowDataCount=0 means the multiplier is currently inactive. B-grade requires OOS IC > 0.02 with p < 0.05.

patch_recommendations.old: bull:     { buy: 1.10, sell: 0.85 },   // 강세 레짐: 매수 신뢰↑, 매도 신뢰↓
patch_recommendations.new: bull:     { buy: 1.06, sell: 0.92 },   // 강세 레짐: 매수 +6% (Ang&Bekaert02 shrinkage), 매도 -8% (Lunde&Timmermann04)

patch_recommendations.old: bear:     { buy: 0.85, sell: 1.10 },   // 약세 레짐: 반대
patch_recommendations.new: bear:     { buy: 0.90, sell: 1.06 },   // 약세 레짐: 매수 -10% (DeMiguel09), 매도 +6%
if (ccsi < 85) adj *= 0.90;  // Lemmon&Portniaguina(2006): -10% shrunk from -12% (5d horizon attenuation)

patch_recommendations.old: adj *= isBuy ? 1.08 : 0.92;   // 극단적 공포 → 매수 유리
patch_recommendations.new: adj *= isBuy ? 1.06 : 0.94;   // 극단적 공포 → 매수 +6% (Pan&Poteshman06 shrinkage)

patch_recommendations.old: adj *= isBuy ? 0.92 : 1.08;   // 극단적 탐욕 → 매도 유리
patch_recommendations.new: adj *= isBuy ? 0.94 : 1.06;   // 극단적 탐욕 → 매도 +6% (Pan&Poteshman06 shrinkage)
var adj = Math.max(0.92, Math.min(1.0, 1 - (globalDelta / 100)));  // EGB96: halved divisor, still ~0.1% with current delta=0.1pp

before_applying_patches: 1. Run conditional backtest: compute pattern WR during HMM bull vs bear periods (requires expanding hmm_regimes.json to full 5-year history)
2. Compute cross-sectional IC of each factor (CCSI, AA spread, PCR, DD) against 5-day forward returns
3. Run walk-forward validation: apply calibrated multipliers to 70/30 train/test split, measure OOS IC improvement
4. Ensure no double-counting: check compound effect when multiple CONF phases fire simultaneously

minimum_evidence_for_B_grade.regime_mult: Conditional WR | regime with n > 500 per regime-direction cell, p < 0.05
minimum_evidence_for_B_grade.ccsi_bear_mult: Conditional WR | CCSI < 85 with n > 200, Spearman IC against forward returns
minimum_evidence_for_B_grade.credit_stress_mult: Conditional WR | AA spread > 1.5% with n > 100 stress-period patterns
minimum_evidence_for_B_grade.pcr_mult: Conditional WR | PCR > 1.3 / PCR < 0.5 with n > 100, Korean-market-specific
